# Machine Learning for Econometricians {.unnumbered}

This open textbook introduces modern machine-learning methods for graduate students in econometrics. The goal is not to replace econometric reasoning with black-box prediction, but to show how machine-learning tools can be used carefully in settings that econometricians actually face: forecasting, financial risk, macroeconomic data, firm-level panels, nonlinear dependence, and distributional uncertainty.

The book assumes a strong econometrics and statistics background, but no prior exposure to machine learning. Concepts such as cross-validation, neural networks, tree-based methods, boosting, conformal prediction, and foundation models for economic text are introduced from first principles and connected to familiar econometric ideas such as likelihood, loss functions, forecast evaluation, time-series dependence, and model selection.

:::{.callout-note appearance="simple" icon=false}
## Who This Book Is For

This book is written for MSc and early PhD students in econometrics, economics, finance, and related quantitative fields.

Readers should be comfortable with probability, mathematical statistics, regression, likelihood-based estimation, and basic time-series methods. No prior knowledge of neural networks, random forests, boosting, or conformal prediction is assumed.
:::

## What Makes This Book Econometric {.unnumbered}

Machine-learning examples are often presented in clean i.i.d. settings. Econometric applications rarely look like that. This book repeatedly emphasizes:

- time-series dependence and real-time forecast evaluation
- heteroskedasticity, tail risk, and predictive distributions
- leakage from inappropriate validation schemes
- nonlinearities and interactions in economic data
<!-- - careful interpretation of predictive models without confusing prediction with causal identification -->

The guiding question throughout is: How can we use flexible machine-learning methods while respecting the information sets, dependence structures, and evaluation problems that arise in econometric work?

## Book Roadmap {.unnumbered}

The chapters are organized into four parts.

| Part | Chapters | Focus |
|------|----------|-------|
| Background | [Information Theory](information_theory.qmd), [Cross Validation](cross_validation.qmd), [Evaluating Predictive Distributions](evaluating_distributions.qmd), [Optimization](optimization.qmd) | Core tools for measuring uncertainty, selecting models, evaluating forecasts, and training flexible models. |
| Neural Networks | [Feed-Forward Networks](feed_forward_nns.qmd), [Recurrent Networks](recurrent_neural_networks.qmd), [LSTM Networks](lstm_neural_networks.qmd), [Empirical Time-Series Exercise](nn_example.qmd), [Distribution Modeling](distributions_via_nns.qmd) | Neural-network models for nonlinear prediction, sequential data, and distributional forecasting. |
| Tree-Based Methods | [Decision Trees](decision_trees.qmd), [Random Forests](random_forests.qmd), [Gradient Boosting](gradient_boosting.qmd), [Advanced Tree-Based Methods](adv_tree_based_methods.qmd) | Interpretable trees, variance reduction by forests, stagewise boosting, and distributional tree methods. |
| Further Topics | [Hyperparameter Optimization](hpo.qmd), [Conformal Prediction](conformal_prediction.qmd), [Foundation Models for Economic Text](foundation_models.qmd), [Data Sets Used in This Book](datasets.qmd) | Modern tools for tuning, uncertainty quantification, text as an econometric input, and the data sources that anchor the empirical examples. |

<!-- ## Suggested Reading Paths {.unnumbered}

If you are using the book for a course, the default path is to read the chapters in order. If you are using it selectively, these paths may be useful:

- **Forecasting path**: [Cross Validation](cross_validation.qmd), [Evaluating Predictive Distributions](evaluating_distributions.qmd), [Recurrent Networks](recurrent_neural_networks.qmd), [LSTM Networks](lstm_neural_networks.qmd), [Distribution Modeling](distributions_via_nns.qmd), [Conformal Prediction](conformal_prediction.qmd).
- **Tree-methods path**: [Decision Trees](decision_trees.qmd), [Random Forests](random_forests.qmd), [Gradient Boosting](gradient_boosting.qmd), [Advanced Tree-Based Methods](adv_tree_based_methods.qmd), [Hyperparameter Optimization](hpo.qmd).
- **Neural-network path**: [Optimization](optimization.qmd), [Feed-Forward Networks](feed_forward_nns.qmd), [Recurrent Networks](recurrent_neural_networks.qmd), [LSTM Networks](lstm_neural_networks.qmd), [Distribution Modeling](distributions_via_nns.qmd).
- **Uncertainty path**: [Information Theory](information_theory.qmd), [Evaluating Predictive Distributions](evaluating_distributions.qmd), [Distribution Modeling](distributions_via_nns.qmd), [Advanced Tree-Based Methods](adv_tree_based_methods.qmd), [Conformal Prediction](conformal_prediction.qmd). -->

## How To Use This Book {.unnumbered}

Each chapter combines formal notation, econometric interpretation, and Python examples. The code chunks are meant to make the ideas concrete, but the mathematical arguments and exercises are designed to stand on their own.

The exercises are intended to be suitable for handwritten exam preparation. They emphasize derivations, forecast-evaluation logic, validation pitfalls, and conceptual distinctions that matter when machine-learning methods are applied to economic data.

## Author and License {.unnumbered}

[**Onno Kleen**](https://onnokleen.com/) is an Assistant Professor of Econometrics at the [Department of Econometrics at Erasmus University Rotterdam](https://www.eur.nl/en/ese/department-econometrics/) and a fellow at the [Tinbergen Institute](https://tinbergen.nl).

This book is licensed under [Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International CC BY-NC-SA 4.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/).
